subject: Physics
question_text: A straight wire of mass 200 g and length 1.5 m carries a current of 2 A. It is suspended in mid-air by a uniform horizontal magnetic field B (Fig). What is the magnitude of the magnetic field?
question_html: A straight wire of mass 200 g and length 1.5 m carries a current of 2 A. It is suspended in mid-air by a uniform horizontal magnetic field B (Fig). What is the magnitude of the magnetic field?
solution_text: We find that there is an upward force F, of magnitude {tex}IlB{/tex} ,. For mid-air suspension, this must be balanced by the force due to gravity: mg = {tex}IlB{/tex} {tex}B=\frac{m g}{I l}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{0.2 \times 9.8}{2 \times 1.5}=0.65 \mathrm{T}{/tex} Note that it would have been sufficient to specify {tex}\frac ml{/tex} , the mass per unit length of the wire. The earth’s magnetic field is approximately 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 T and we have ignored it.
solution_html:
We find that there is an upward force F, of magnitude {tex}IlB{/tex},. For mid-air suspension, this must be balanced by the force due to gravity: mg = {tex}IlB{/tex}
{tex}B=\frac{m g}{I l}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.2 \times 9.8}{2 \times 1.5}=0.65 \mathrm{T}{/tex}
Note that it would have been sufficient to specify {tex}\frac ml{/tex}, the mass per unit length of the wire. The earth’s magnetic field is approximately 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 T and we have ignored it.

subject: Physics
question_text: If the magnetic field is parallel to the positive y-axis and the charged particle is moving along the positive x-axis (Fig.), which way would the Lorentz force be for an electron (negative charge), a proton (positive charge).
question_html:
If the magnetic field is parallel to the positive y-axis and the charged particle is moving along the positive x-axis (Fig.), which way would the Lorentz force be for

  1. an electron (negative charge),
  2. a proton (positive charge).
solution_text: The velocity v of the particle is along the x-axis, while B, the magnetic field is along the y-axis, so v {tex}\times{/tex} B is along the z-axis (screw rule or right-hand thumb rule). So, depending upon the charge force is given by: for electron it will be along –z axis. for a positive charge (proton) the force is along +z axis.
solution_html:
The velocity v of the particle is along the x-axis, while B, the magnetic field is along the y-axis, so v {tex}\times{/tex} B is along the z-axis (screw rule or right-hand thumb rule). So, depending upon the charge force is given by:

  1. for electron it will be along –z axis.
  2. for a positive charge (proton) the force is along +z axis.

subject: Physics
question_text: What is the radius of the path of an electron (mass 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg and charge 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 C) moving at a speed of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m/s in a magnetic field of 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 T perpendicular to it? What is its frequency? Calculate its energy in keV. ( 1 eV = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J).
question_html: What is the radius of the path of an electron (mass 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg and charge 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 C) moving at a speed of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m/s in a magnetic field of 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4 T perpendicular to it? What is its frequency? Calculate its energy in keV. ( 1 eV = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 J).
solution_text: here m = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg and B = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T r = {tex}\frac{m v }{ (qB)} =\frac {9 \times 10^{–31} \ kg \ \times\ 3 \times 10^7 \ m s^{–1} }{ ( 1.6 \times 10^{–19 }\ C\ \times \ 6\times 10^{–4}\ T)}{/tex} = 28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –2 m = 28 cm {tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac v { (2 \pi r)}{/tex} = 17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 s –1 = 17 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 Hz =17 MHz. E = ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} )mv 2 = ( {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ) 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg {tex}\times{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 m 2 /s 2 = 40.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –17 J {tex}\approx {/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –16 J = 2.5 keV.
solution_html:
here m = 9{tex}\times{/tex}10-31kg  and B = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T
r = {tex}\frac{m v }{ (qB)} =\frac {9 \times 10^{–31} \ kg \ \times\ 3 \times 10^7 \ m s^{–1} }{ ( 1.6 \times 10^{–19 }\ C\ \times \ 6\times 10^{–4}\ T)}{/tex}
= 28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–2 m = 28 cm
{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac v { (2 \pi r)}{/tex} = 17 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 s–1 = 17 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 Hz =17 MHz.
E = ({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} )mv2 = ({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}) 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg {tex}\times{/tex} 9 {tex}\times{/tex}1014 m2/s2 = 40.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–17 J
{tex}\approx {/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–16 J = 2.5 keV.

subject: Physics
question_text: An element {tex}\Delta \vec{l}=\Delta x \hat{i}{/tex} is placed at the origin and carries a large current I = 10 A (Fig.). What is the magnetic field on the y-axis at a distance of 0.5 m? {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 1 cm.
question_html: An element  {tex}\Delta \vec{l}=\Delta x \hat{i}{/tex} is placed at the origin and carries a large current I = 10 A (Fig.). What is the magnetic field on the y-axis at a distance of 0.5 m?  {tex}\Delta{/tex}x = 1 cm.
solution_text: we know that, {tex}|\mathrm{dB}|=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I \mathrm{d} l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}dl{/tex} = {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 1cm = 10 -2 m, I = 10 A, r = 0.5 m, {tex}\mu_{0} / 4 \pi=10^{-7} \frac{T m}{A}{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°; sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 {tex}|\mathrm{dB}|=\frac{10^{-7} \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{25 \times 10^{-2}}=4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{T}{/tex} The direction of the field is in the +z-direction. This is so since, {tex}\mathrm{d}{l} \times {r}=\Delta x \hat{{i}} \times y \hat{{j}}=y \Delta x(\hat{{i}} \times \hat{{j}})=y \Delta x \hat{{k}}{/tex} We remind you of the following cyclic property of cross-products, {tex}\hat{{i}} \times \hat{{j}}=\hat{{k}} ; \hat{{j}} \times \hat{{k}}=\hat{{i}} ; \hat{{k}} \times \hat{{i}}=\hat{{j}}{/tex} Note that the field is small in magnitude.
solution_html:
we know that, {tex}|\mathrm{dB}|=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I \mathrm{d} l \sin \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}dl{/tex} = {tex}\Delta{/tex} x = 1cm = 10-2 m, I = 10 A, r = 0.5 m, {tex}\mu_{0} / 4 \pi=10^{-7} \frac{T m}{A}{/tex}
{tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°; sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1
{tex}|\mathrm{dB}|=\frac{10^{-7} \times 10 \times 10^{-2}}{25 \times 10^{-2}}=4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{T}{/tex}
The direction of the field is in the +z-direction. This is so since,
{tex}\mathrm{d}{l} \times {r}=\Delta x \hat{{i}} \times y \hat{{j}}=y \Delta x(\hat{{i}} \times \hat{{j}})=y \Delta x \hat{{k}}{/tex}
We remind you of the following cyclic property of cross-products,
{tex}\hat{{i}} \times \hat{{j}}=\hat{{k}} ; \hat{{j}} \times \hat{{k}}=\hat{{i}} ; \hat{{k}} \times \hat{{i}}=\hat{{j}}{/tex}
Note that the field is small in magnitude.

subject: Physics
question_text: A straight wire carrying a current of 12 A is bent into a semi-circular arc of radius 2.0 cm as shown in Fig. (a). Consider the magnetic field B at the centre of the arc. What is the magnetic field due to the straight segments? In what way the contribution to B from the semicircle differs from that of a circular loop and in what way does it resemble? Would your answer be different if the wire were bent into a semi-circular arc of the same radius but in the opposite way as shown in Fig. (b)?
question_html:
A straight wire carrying a current of 12 A is bent into a semi-circular arc of radius 2.0 cm as shown in Fig. (a). Consider the magnetic field B at the centre of the arc.

  1. What is the magnetic field due to the straight segments?
  2. In what way the contribution to B from the semicircle differs from that of a circular loop and in what way does it resemble?
  3. Would your answer be different if the wire were bent into a semi-circular arc of the same radius but in the opposite way as shown in Fig. (b)?
solution_text: dl and r for each element of the straight segments are parallel. Therefore, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = 0. Straight segments do not contribute to |B|. For all segments of the semicircular arc, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r are all parallel to each other (into the plane of the paper). All such contributions add up in magnitude. Hence the direction of B for a semicircular arc is given by the right-hand rule and magnitude is half that of a circular loop. Thus B is 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –4 T normal to the plane of the paper going into it. Same magnitude of B but its direction will be normal to the plane of paper directed outwards.
solution_html:
1. dl and r for each element of the straight segments are parallel. Therefore, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r = 0. Straight segments do not contribute to |B|.
  2. For all segments of the semicircular arc, dl {tex}\times{/tex} r are all parallel to each other (into the plane of the paper). All such contributions add up in magnitude. Hence the direction of B for a semicircular arc is given by the right-hand rule and magnitude is half that of a circular loop. Thus B is 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–4 T normal to the plane of the paper going into it.
  3. Same magnitude of B but its direction will be normal to the plane of paper directed outwards.

subject: Physics
question_text: Consider a tightly wound 100 turn coil of radius 10 cm, carrying a current of 1 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of the coil?
question_html: Consider a tightly wound 100 turn coil of radius 10 cm, carrying a current of 1 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of the coil?
solution_text: since the coil is tightly wound, we may take each circular element to have the same radius R = 10 cm = 0.1 m. The number of turns N = 100. The magnitude of the magnetic field is, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-1}}=2 \pi \times 10^{-4}=6.28 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{T}{/tex}
solution_html:
since the coil is tightly wound, we may take each circular element to have the same radius R = 10 cm = 0.1 m. The number of turns N = 100. The magnitude of the magnetic field is,
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-1}}=2 \pi \times 10^{-4}=6.28 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{T}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Calculate the magnetic field in the region r < a and r > a.
question_html: Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Calculate the magnetic field in the region r < a and r > a.
solution_text: Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r I e = Current enclosed by the loop = I The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire B (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r) = {tex}\mu {/tex} 0 I {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex} {tex}B \propto \frac{1}{r} \quad(r>a){/tex} Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1. For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r, L = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r Now the current enclosed Ie is not I, but is less than this value. Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is, {tex}I_{e}=I\left(\frac{\pi r^{2}}{\pi a^{2}}\right)=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} Using Ampere’s law, {tex}B(2 \pi r)=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex} {tex}B=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a^{2}}\right) r{/tex} {tex}B \propto r \quad(r<a){/tex} The figure shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential to the respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule described earlier in this section. This example possesses the required symmetry so that Ampere’s law can be applied readily.
solution_html:
1. Figure shows a long straight wire of a circular cross-section (radius a) carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Consider the case r > a. The Amperian loop, labelled 2, is a circle concentric with the cross-section. For this loop,
    L = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
    Ie = Current enclosed by the loop = I
    The result is the familiar expression for a long straight wire
    B (2{tex}\pi{/tex} r) = {tex}\mu {/tex}0I
    {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}{/tex}
    {tex}B \propto \frac{1}{r} \quad(r>a){/tex}
  2. Consider the case r < a. The Amperian loop is a circle labelled 1. For this loop, taking the radius of the circle to be r, L = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r Now the current enclosed Ie
    is not I, but is less than this value. Since the current distribution is uniform, the current enclosed is, {tex}I_{e}=I\left(\frac{\pi r^{2}}{\pi a^{2}}\right)=\frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
    Using Ampere’s law, {tex}B(2 \pi r)=\mu_{0} \frac{I r^{2}}{a^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}B=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 a^{2}}\right) r{/tex}
    {tex}B \propto r \quad(r<a){/tex}

    The figure shows a plot of the magnitude of B with distance r from the centre of the wire. The direction of the field is tangential to the respective circular loop (1 or 2) and given by the right-hand rule described earlier in this section. This example possesses the required symmetry so that Ampere’s law can be applied readily.

subject: Physics
question_text: A solenoid of length 0.5 m has a radius of 1 cm and is made up of 500 turns. It carries a current of 5 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid?
question_html: A solenoid of length 0.5 m has a radius of 1 cm and is made up of 500 turns. It carries a current of 5 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid?
solution_text: The number of turns per unit length is, {tex}n=\frac{500}{0.5}=1000{/tex} turns/m The length l = 0.5 m and radius r = 0.01 m. Thus, l/r = 50 i.e., l >> r Hence, we can use the long solenoid formula, namely, {tex}B=\mu_{0} n I{/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 T
solution_html:
The number of turns per unit length is,
{tex}n=\frac{500}{0.5}=1000{/tex} turns/m
The length l = 0.5 m and radius r = 0.01 m. Thus, l/r = 50 i.e., l >> r
Hence, we can use the long solenoid formula, namely,
{tex}B=\mu_{0} n I{/tex}
= 4{tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10–7 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
= 6.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 T

subject: Physics
question_text: The horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field at a certain place is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 T and the direction of the field is from the geographic south to the geographic north. A very long straight conductor is carrying a steady current of 1A. What is the force per unit length on it when it is placed on a horizontal table and the direction of the current is east to west; south to north?
question_html:
The horizontal component of the earth’s magnetic field at a certain place is 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 T and the direction of the field is from the geographic south to the geographic north. A very long straight conductor is carrying a steady current of 1A. What is the force per unit length on it when it is placed on a horizontal table and the direction of the current is

  1. east to west;
  2. south to north?
solution_text: {tex}\mathbf{F}=I \mathbf{L} \times \mathbf{B}{/tex} {tex}F=I BL \sin \theta{/tex} The force per unit length is f = {tex}\frac FL{/tex} = I B sin {tex}\theta{/tex} When the current is flowing from east to west, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90° Hence, sin 90° = 1 thus the force per unit length is given by f = I B = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –5 N m –1 This is larger than the value 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –7 Nm –1 quoted in the definition of the ampere. Hence it is important to eliminate the effect of the earth’s magnetic field and other stray fields while standardizing the ampere. The direction of the force is downwards. This direction may be obtained by the directional property of cross product of vectors. When the current is flowing from south to north, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o and sin0 0 =0 f = 0 Hence there is no force on the conductor.
solution_html:
{tex}\mathbf{F}=I \mathbf{L} \times \mathbf{B}{/tex}
{tex}F=I BL \sin \theta{/tex}
The force per unit length is
f = {tex}\frac FL{/tex} = I B sin{tex}\theta{/tex}

  1. When the current is flowing from east to west,
    {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90°
    Hence, sin 90° = 1
    thus the force per unit length is given by f = I B
    = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–5 N m–1
    This is larger than the value 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–7 Nm–1 quoted in the definition of the ampere. Hence it is important to eliminate the effect of the earth’s magnetic field and other stray fields while standardizing the ampere. The direction of the force is downwards. This direction may be obtained by the directional property of cross product of vectors.
  2. When the current is flowing from south to north,
    {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o and sin00=0
    f = 0
    Hence there is no force on the conductor.

subject: Physics
question_text: A 100 turn closely wound circular coil of radius 10 cm carries a current of 3.2 A. What is the field at the centre of the coil? What is the magnetic moment of this coil? The coil is placed in a vertical plane and is free to rotate about a horizontal axis which coincides with its diameter. A uniform magnetic field of 2T in the horizontal direction exists such that initially the axis of the coil is in the direction of the field. The coil rotates through an angle of 90° under the influence of the magnetic field. What are the magnitudes of the torques on the coil in the initial and final position? What is the angular speed acquired by the coil when it has rotated by 90°? The moment of inertia of the coil is 0.1 kg m 2 .
question_html:
A 100 turn closely wound circular coil of radius 10 cm carries a current of 3.2 A.

  1. What is the field at the centre of the coil?
  2. What is the magnetic moment of this coil? The coil is placed in a vertical plane and is free to rotate about a horizontal axis which coincides with its diameter. A uniform magnetic field of 2T in the horizontal direction exists such that initially the axis of the coil is in the direction of the field. The coil rotates through an angle of 90° under the influence of the magnetic field.
  3. What are the magnitudes of the torques on the coil in the initial and final position?
  4. What is the angular speed acquired by the coil when it has rotated by 90°? The moment of inertia of the coil is 0.1 kg m2.
solution_text: {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}{/tex} Here, N = 100; I = 3.2 A, and R = 0.1 m. Hence, {tex}B=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 3.2}{2 \times 10^{-1}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-5} \times 10}{2 \times 10^{-1}}{/tex} (using {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3.2 = 10) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –3 T The direction is given by the right-hand thumb rule. The magnetic moment m = NIA = NI {tex}\pi{/tex} r 2 = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –2 = 10 A m 2 The direction is once again given by the right-hand thumb rule. {tex}\tau=|\mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{B}|{/tex} = mBsin {tex}\theta{/tex} Initially, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0. Thus, initial torque {tex}{{\tau }_{i}}{/tex} = 0. Finally, {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} /2 (or 90º). Thus, final torque {tex}{{\tau }_{f}}{/tex} = m B = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 20 N m. From Newton’s second law, moment of inertia {tex}I{/tex} = 0.1 kg m 2 {tex}I \frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} t}=m B \sin \theta{/tex} where g is the moment of inertia of the coil. From chain rule, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \omega{/tex} Using this, {tex}I \omega \mathrm{d} \omega=m B \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} Integrating from θ = 0 to θ = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} , {tex}I \int_{0}^{\omega_{f}} \omega \mathrm{d} \omega=m B \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex} {tex}I \frac{\omega_{f}^{2}}{2}=-m B \cos \theta\left[_{0}^{\pi / 2}=m B\right.{/tex} hence, angular speed acquired by the coil is given by :- {tex}\omega_{f}=\left(\frac{2 m B}{I}\right)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{2 \times 20}{10^{-1}}\right)^{1 / 2}=20 rads^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}B=\frac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}{/tex}
    Here, N = 100; I = 3.2 A, and R = 0.1 m. Hence,
    {tex}B=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{2} \times 3.2}{2 \times 10^{-1}}=\frac{4 \times 10^{-5} \times 10}{2 \times 10^{-1}}{/tex} (using {tex}\pi{/tex}  {tex}\times{/tex} 3.2 = 10)
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–3 T
    The direction is given by the right-hand thumb rule.
  2. The magnetic moment
    m = NIA = NI{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–2 = 10 A m2
    The direction is once again given by the right-hand thumb rule.
  3. {tex}\tau=|\mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{B}|{/tex}
    = mBsin{tex}\theta{/tex}
    Initially, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0. Thus, initial torque {tex}{{\tau }_{i}}{/tex} = 0. Finally, {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}/2 (or 90º).
    Thus, final torque {tex}{{\tau }_{f}}{/tex} = m B = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 20 N m.
  4. From Newton’s second law, moment of inertia {tex}I{/tex} = 0.1 kg m 2
    {tex}I \frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} t}=m B \sin \theta{/tex}
    where g is the moment of inertia of the coil. From chain rule,
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} t}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \omega}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \omega{/tex}
    Using this,
    {tex}I \omega \mathrm{d} \omega=m B \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex}
    Integrating from θ = 0 to θ = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex},
    {tex}I \int_{0}^{\omega_{f}} \omega \mathrm{d} \omega=m B \int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta{/tex}
    {tex}I \frac{\omega_{f}^{2}}{2}=-m B \cos \theta\left[_{0}^{\pi / 2}=m B\right.{/tex}      hence, angular speed acquired by the coil is given by :-
    {tex}\omega_{f}=\left(\frac{2 m B}{I}\right)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{2 \times 20}{10^{-1}}\right)^{1 / 2}=20 rads^{-1}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A current-carrying circular loop lies on a smooth horizontal plane. Can a uniform magnetic field be set up in such a manner that the loop turns around itself (i.e., turns about the vertical axis). A current-carrying circular loop is located in a uniform external magnetic field. If the loop is free to turn, what is its orientation of stable equilibrium? Show that in this orientation, the flux of the total field (external field + field produced by the loop) is maximum. A loop of irregular shape carrying current is located in an external magnetic field. If the wire is flexible, why does it change to a circular shape?
question_html:
1. A current-carrying circular loop lies on a smooth horizontal plane. Can a uniform magnetic field be set up in such a manner that the loop turns around itself (i.e., turns about the vertical axis).
  2. A current-carrying circular loop is located in a uniform external magnetic field. If the loop is free to turn, what is its orientation of stable equilibrium? Show that in this orientation, the flux of the total field (external field + field produced by the loop) is maximum.
  3. A loop of irregular shape carrying current is located in an external magnetic field. If the wire is flexible, why does it change to a circular shape?
solution_text: No, because that would require {tex}\tau{/tex} to be in the vertical direction. But {tex}\tau{/tex} = I A {tex}\times{/tex} B , and since A of the horizontal loop is in the vertical direction, t would be in the plane of the loop for any B . Orientation of stable equilibrium is one where the area vector A of the loop is in the direction of external magnetic field. In this orientation, the magnetic field produced by the loop is in the same direction as external field, both normal to the plane of the loop, thus giving rise to maximum flux of the total field. It assumes circular shape with its plane normal to the field to maximise flux, since for a given perimeter, a circle encloses greater area than any other shape.
solution_html:
1. No, because that would require {tex}\tau{/tex} to be in the vertical direction. But {tex}\tau{/tex} = I A {tex}\times{/tex} B, and since A of the horizontal loop is in the vertical direction, t would be in the plane of the loop for any B.
  2. Orientation of stable equilibrium is one where the area vector A of the loop is in the direction of external magnetic field. In this orientation, the magnetic field produced by the loop is in the same direction as external field, both normal to the plane of the loop, thus giving rise to maximum flux of the total field.
  3. It assumes circular shape with its plane normal to the field to maximise flux, since for a given perimeter, a circle encloses greater area than any other shape.

subject: Physics
question_text: In the circuit (Fig.) the current is to be measured. What is the value of the current if the ammeter shown is a galvanometer with a resistance R G = 60.00 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ; is a galvanometer described in (a) but converted to an ammeter by a shunt resistance r s = 0.02 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ; is an ideal ammeter with zero resistance?
question_html:
In the circuit (Fig.) the current is to be measured. What is the value of the current if the ammeter shown

  1. is a galvanometer with a resistance RG = 60.00 {tex}\Omega{/tex};
  2. is a galvanometer described in (a) but converted to an ammeter by a shunt resistance rs = 0.02 {tex}\Omega{/tex};
  3. is an ideal ammeter with zero resistance?
solution_text: Total resistance in the circuit is, R G = 60.00 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . Hence, I = {tex}e/(r+g){/tex} = {tex}\frac 3{63}{/tex} = 0.048 A The resistance of the galvanometer converted to an ammeter is, {tex}\frac{R_{C} r_{s}}{R_{G}+r_{s}}=\frac{60 \Omega \times 0.02 \Omega}{(60+0.02) \Omega}=0.02 \Omega{/tex} The total resistance in the circuit is, {tex}0.02 \Omega+3 \Omega=3.02 \Omega{/tex} . Hence, total current is given by I = {tex}\frac 3{3.02}{/tex} = 0.99 A For the ideal ammeter with zero resistance, I = {tex}\frac 33{/tex} = 1.00 A
solution_html:
1. Total resistance in the circuit is, RG = 60.00{tex}\Omega{/tex}. Hence, I ={tex}e/(r+g){/tex}= {tex}\frac 3{63}{/tex} = 0.048 A
  2. The resistance of the galvanometer converted to an ammeter is, {tex}\frac{R_{C} r_{s}}{R_{G}+r_{s}}=\frac{60 \Omega \times 0.02 \Omega}{(60+0.02) \Omega}=0.02 \Omega{/tex}
    The total resistance in the circuit is, {tex}0.02 \Omega+3 \Omega=3.02 \Omega{/tex}. Hence, total current is given by  I = {tex}\frac 3{3.02}{/tex} = 0.99 A
  3. For the ideal ammeter with zero resistance, I = {tex}\frac 33{/tex} = 1.00 A

subject: Physics
question_text: A circular coil of wire consisting of 100 turns, each of radius 8.0 cm carries a current of 0.40 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field B at the centre of the coil?
question_html: A circular coil of wire consisting of 100 turns, each of radius 8.0 cm carries a current of 0.40 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field B at the centre of the coil?
solution_text: Given, I = 0.40 A, r = 8.0 cm {tex} = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}}m{/tex} , n = 100 Using Biot Savart law, we find that magnetic field at the centre of a coil is given by, {tex}B=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{2\pi nI}r=\frac{\mu_0nI}{2r}=\frac{4\pi\times10^{-7}\times100\times0.4}{2\times8.0\times10^{-2}}{/tex} {tex} = 3.1 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, I = 0.40 A, r = 8.0 cm {tex} = 8 \times {10^{ - 2}}m{/tex}, n = 100
Using Biot Savart law, we find that magnetic field at the centre of a coil is given by,
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{2\pi nI}r=\frac{\mu_0nI}{2r}=\frac{4\pi\times10^{-7}\times100\times0.4}{2\times8.0\times10^{-2}}{/tex}
{tex} = 3.1 \times {10^{ - 4}}T{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A long straight wire carries a current of 35 A. What is the magnitude of the field B at a point 20 cm from the wire?
question_html: A long straight wire carries a current of 35 A. What is the magnitude of the field B at a point 20 cm from the wire?
solution_text: Given, I = 35 A, r = 20 cm, = 0.2 m {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 35}}{{2\pi \times 0.20}}{/tex} {tex} = 3.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}T{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, I = 35 A, r = 20 cm, = 0.2 m
{tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 35}}{{2\pi \times 0.20}}{/tex}
{tex} = 3.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}T{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A long straight wire in the horizontal plane carries a current of 50 A in north to south direction. Give the magnitude and direction of B at a point 2.5 m east of the wire.
question_html: A long straight wire in the horizontal plane carries a current of 50 A in north to south direction. Give the magnitude and direction of B at a point 2.5 m east of the wire.
solution_text: Given, I = 50 A, r = 2.5 m B = ? As {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 50}}{{2\pi \times 2.5}}{/tex} {tex} = 4 \times {10^{ - 6}}T{/tex} Applying right hand thumb rule to find the direction of M.F. in east direction of wire it comes out upward direction.
solution_html:
Given, I = 50 A, r = 2.5 m
B = ?
As {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 50}}{{2\pi \times 2.5}}{/tex}
{tex} = 4 \times {10^{ - 6}}T{/tex}
Applying right hand thumb rule to find the direction of M.F. in east direction of wire it comes out upward direction.

subject: Physics
question_text: A horizontal overhead power line carries a current of 90 A in east to west direction. What is the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field due to the current 1.5 m below the line?
question_html: A horizontal overhead power line carries a current of 90 A in east to west direction. What is the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field due to the current 1.5 m below the line?
solution_text: It is an example of magnetic field due to current in a wire of infinite length. {tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 90}}{{2\pi \times 1.5}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{180}}{{1.5}} \times {10^{ - 7}} = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}T{/tex} Applying the right hand thumb rule, we find that the magnetic field at the observation point is directed towards south.
solution_html:
It is an example of magnetic field due to current in a wire of infinite length.
{tex}B = \frac{{{\mu _0}I}}{{2\pi r}} = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 90}}{{2\pi \times 1.5}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{180}}{{1.5}} \times {10^{ - 7}} = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}T{/tex}
Applying the right hand thumb rule, we find that the magnetic field at the observation point is directed towards south.

subject: Physics
question_text: What is the magnitude of magnetic force per unit length on a wire carrying a current of 8 A and making an angle of 30° with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.15T.
question_html: What is the magnitude of magnetic force per unit length on a wire carrying a current of 8 A and making an angle of 30° with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.15T.
solution_text: Here, B = 0.15T; I = 8A; l = 1m and {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o Now, F = {tex}\mathrm{B} \mathrm{I} l \sin \theta{/tex} = 0.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o = 0.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 0.6 {tex}\frac{N}{m}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, B = 0.15T; I = 8A; l = 1m and {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o
Now, F = {tex}\mathrm{B} \mathrm{I} l \sin \theta{/tex} = 0.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30o
= 0.15 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 0.6 {tex}\frac{N}{m}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A 3.0 cm wire carrying a current of 10 A is placed inside a solenoid perpendicular to its axis. The magnetic field inside the solenoid is given to be 0.27 T. What is the magnetic force on the wire?
question_html: A 3.0 cm wire carrying a current of 10 A is placed inside a solenoid perpendicular to its axis. The magnetic field inside the solenoid is given to be 0.27 T. What is the magnetic force on the wire?
solution_text: Here, l = 3.0 cm = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m I = 10 A, B = 0.27 T, {tex}\theta = 90^\circ{/tex} , F = ? By the formula {tex}F = BH\sin \theta{/tex} {tex} = 0.27 \times 10 \times (3 \times {10^{ - 2}}) \times sin90^\circ {/tex} {tex} = 0.27 \times 10 \times 3 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times 1{/tex} {tex}F = 8.1 \times {10^{ - 2}}N{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, l = 3.0 cm = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2m
I = 10 A, B = 0.27 T, {tex}\theta = 90^\circ{/tex}, F = ?
By the formula
{tex}F = BH\sin \theta{/tex}
{tex} = 0.27 \times 10 \times (3 \times {10^{ - 2}}) \times sin90^\circ {/tex}
{tex} = 0.27 \times 10 \times 3 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times 1{/tex}
{tex}F = 8.1 \times {10^{ - 2}}N{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: Two long and parallel straight wires A and B carrying currents of 8.0 A and 5.0 A in the same direction are separated by a distance of 4.0 cm. Estimate the force on a 10 cm section of wire A.
question_html: Two long and parallel straight wires A and B carrying currents of 8.0 A and 5.0 A in the same direction are separated by a distance of 4.0 cm. Estimate the force on a 10 cm section of wire A.
solution_text: Given, I 1 = 8.0, I 2 = 5A r = 4.0 cm = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m l = 10 cm = 0.1 m F = ? Force on length l, {tex}F = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{{{I_1}{I_2}l}}{r}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{(4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}}) \times 8 \times 5 \times 0.1}}{{2\pi \times 0.04}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 5}}N{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, I1 = 8.0, I2 = 5A
r = 4.0 cm = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2m
l = 10 cm = 0.1 m
F = ?
Force on length l,
{tex}F = \frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}\frac{{{I_1}{I_2}l}}{r}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{(4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}}) \times 8 \times 5 \times 0.1}}{{2\pi \times 0.04}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 5}}N{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A closely wound solenoid 80 cm long has 5 layers of windings of 400 turns each. The diameter of the solenoid is 1.8 cm. If the current carried is 8.0 A, estimate the magnitude of B inside the solenoid near its centre.
question_html: A closely wound solenoid 80 cm long has 5 layers of windings of 400 turns each. The diameter of the solenoid is 1.8 cm. If the current carried is 8.0 A, estimate the magnitude of B inside the solenoid near its centre.
solution_text: Here, l= 80 cm = 0.8 m, N = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 400 = 2000 I = 8.0 A, D = 1.8 cm, n = no. of turns per unit length Magnitude of magnetic field inside a solenoid near its center {tex}n = \frac{{Total\;turns}}{{length}}{/tex} {tex}n = \frac{{2000}}{{0.80}}{/tex} Magnitude of magnetic field induction at a point well inside the solenoid is, {tex}B = {\mu _0}nI = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 2000 \times 8.0}}{{0.80}}{/tex} {tex} = 8\pi \times {10^{ - 3}}T = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}T{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, l= 80 cm = 0.8 m, N = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 400 = 2000
I = 8.0 A, D = 1.8 cm, n = no. of turns per unit length
Magnitude of magnetic field inside a solenoid near its center
{tex}n = \frac{{Total\;turns}}{{length}}{/tex}
{tex}n = \frac{{2000}}{{0.80}}{/tex}
Magnitude of magnetic field induction at a point well inside the solenoid is, {tex}B = {\mu _0}nI = \frac{{4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 2000 \times 8.0}}{{0.80}}{/tex}
{tex} = 8\pi \times {10^{ - 3}}T = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}T{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_text: A square coil of side 10 cm consists of 20 turns and carries a current of 12 A. The coil is suspended vertically and the normal to the plane of the coil makes an angle of 30º with the direction of a uniform horizontal magnetic field of magnitude 0.80 T. What is the magnitude of torque experienced by the coil?
question_html: A square coil of side 10 cm consists of 20 turns and carries a current of 12 A. The coil is suspended vertically and the normal to the plane of the coil makes an angle of 30º with the direction of a uniform horizontal magnetic field of magnitude 0.80 T. What is the magnitude of torque experienced by the coil?
solution_text: Given: Length of side of square, L = 10 cm Number of turns, n = 20 Current through the square coil, I = 12 A Angle between the normal to the coil and uniform magnetic field, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30° Magnitude of magnetic field, B = 0.80 T The torque experienced by the coil in a magnetic field is given by, {tex}\tau{/tex} = n {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} sin( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) …(1) Where, n = number of turns consist by square B = Strength of magnetic field I = Current through the coil A = Area of cross-section of coil A = L 2 = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 0.01m 2 …(2) θ = Angle between normal to cross-section of coil and magnetic field Now by putting the values in equation (1), we get {tex}\tau{/tex} = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.80T {tex}\times{/tex} 12A {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01m 2 {tex}\times{/tex} sin30° and the value of sin30 0 =1/2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.96 Nm {tex}\therefore{/tex} the magnitude torque experienced by the coil is 0.96 N-m. Hence the torque experienced by the square coil is 0.96 Nm.
solution_html:
Given:
Length of side of square, L = 10 cm
Number of turns, n = 20
Current through the square coil, I = 12 A
Angle between the normal to the coil and uniform magnetic field, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30°
Magnitude of magnetic field, B = 0.80 T

The torque experienced by the coil in a magnetic field is given by,
{tex}\tau{/tex} = n {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} I {tex}\times{/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} sin({tex}\theta{/tex}) …(1)
Where,
n = number of turns consist by square 
B = Strength of magnetic field
I = Current through the coil
A = Area of cross-section of coil
A = L2 = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 = 0.01m2 …(2)
θ = Angle between normal to cross-section of coil and magnetic field
Now by putting the values in equation (1), we get
{tex}\tau{/tex} = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.80T {tex}\times{/tex} 12A {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01m2 {tex}\times{/tex} sin30° and the value of sin300=1/2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\tau{/tex}= 0.96 Nm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the magnitude torque experienced by the coil is 0.96 N-m.
Hence the torque experienced by the square coil is 0.96 Nm.

subject: Physics
question_text: Two moving coil meters, M 1 and M 2 have the following particulars: R 1 = 10 {tex}\Omega{/tex} ; N 1 = 30, A 1 = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m 2 , B 1 = 0.25T, R 2 = 14 {tex}\Omega{/tex} , N 2 = 42, A 2 = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m 2 , B 2 = 0.50T (The spring constants k are identical for the two meters). Determine the ratio of current sensitivity and voltage sensitivity of M 2 and M 1 .
question_html:
Two moving coil meters, M1 and M2 have the following particulars: R1 = 10{tex}\Omega{/tex}; N1 = 30, A1 = 3.6{tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m2, B1 = 0.25T, R2 = 14{tex}\Omega{/tex}, N2 = 42, A2 = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3m2, B2 = 0.50T
(The spring constants k are identical for the two meters).
Determine the ratio of

  1. current sensitivity and
  2. voltage sensitivity of M2 and M1.
solution_text: Current sensitivity of first meter, {tex}A = \frac{\theta }{I} = \frac{{{B_1}{A_1}{N_1}}}{k} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{0.25 \times 3.6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 30}}{k}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{27 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex} ...(i) Current sensitivity of second meter, {tex}B = \frac{\theta }{I} = \frac{{{B_2}{A_2}{N_2}}}{k}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.50 \times 1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 42}}{k}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{37.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex} ...(ii) Ratio of current sensitivity {tex}\left( {\frac{B}{A}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{{37.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex} {tex}\times\frac{k}{27 \times {10}^{ - 3}}{/tex} = 1.4 Voltage sensitivity of first meter {tex} = \frac{\theta }{V} = \frac{\theta }{{I \cdot R}} = \frac{{27 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{k \times 10}} = \frac{{2.7 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex} Voltage sensitivity of second meter {tex} = \frac{\theta }{{R \cdot I}} = \frac{{37.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{k \times 10}} = \frac{{2.7 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex} Hence, the ratio of voltage sensitivity = 1
solution_html:
1. Current sensitivity of first meter,
    {tex}A = \frac{\theta }{I} = \frac{{{B_1}{A_1}{N_1}}}{k} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{0.25 \times 3.6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 30}}{k}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{27 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex} ...(i)
    Current sensitivity of second meter,
    {tex}B = \frac{\theta }{I} = \frac{{{B_2}{A_2}{N_2}}}{k}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.50 \times 1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 42}}{k}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{37.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex} ...(ii)
    Ratio of current sensitivity {tex}\left( {\frac{B}{A}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{{37.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex}{tex}\times\frac{k}{27 \times {10}^{ - 3}}{/tex} = 1.4
  2. Voltage sensitivity of first meter
    {tex} = \frac{\theta }{V} = \frac{\theta }{{I \cdot R}} = \frac{{27 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{k \times 10}} = \frac{{2.7 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex}
    Voltage sensitivity of second meter
    {tex} = \frac{\theta }{{R \cdot I}} = \frac{{37.8 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{k \times 10}} = \frac{{2.7 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{k}{/tex}
    Hence, the ratio of voltage sensitivity = 1

subject: Physics
question_text: In a chamber, a uniform magnetic field of 6.5 G (1 G = 10 –4 T) is maintained. An electron is shot into the field with a speed of 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms –1 normal to the field. Explain why the path of the electron is a circle. Determine the radius of the circular orbit. (e = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 C, m e = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –31 kg)
question_html: In a chamber, a uniform magnetic field of 6.5 G (1 G = 10–4 T) is maintained. An electron is shot into the field with a speed of 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms–1 normal to the field. Explain why the path of the electron is a circle. Determine the radius of the circular orbit. (e = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 C, me = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–31 kg)
solution_text: Given: Magnetic field strength, B = 6.5 G = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T Initial velocity of electron = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 Angle between the initial velocity of electron and magnetic field, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90 0 Force on the electron, F e = e {tex}\times{/tex} V {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ...(1) Where, e = charge on electron V = velocity of electron B = Magnetic field strength {tex}\theta{/tex} = angle between direction of velocity and magnetic field By putting the values in equation (1), we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F e = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C {tex}\times{/tex} 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ms -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -4 T {tex}\times{/tex} sin 90 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F e = 4.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 N Force acting on an electron is always perpendicular to direction of motion and towards center of the circular path and hence its path will be a circle. This force serves as the centripetal force, which explains the circular trajectory of the electron. Centripetal force F c = {tex}\frac {m v^2}{r}{/tex} ...(2) By equating equation (1) and equation (2) we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{m \times v^{2}}{r}=4.99 \times 10^{-16} N{/tex} ⇒ {tex}r=\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg} \times\left(4.8 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)^{2}}{4.99 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{N}}{/tex} r = 4.2 cm. Hence the radius of the path of electron shot into the magnetic field is 4.2 cm.
solution_html:
Given:
Magnetic field strength, B = 6.5 G = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T
Initial velocity of electron = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms-1
Angle between the initial velocity of electron and magnetic field, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 900

Force on the electron,
Fe = e {tex}\times{/tex} V {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex} ...(1)
Where,
e = charge on electron
V = velocity of electron
B = Magnetic field strength
{tex}\theta{/tex} = angle between direction of velocity and magnetic field
By putting the values in equation (1), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  Fe = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C {tex}\times{/tex} 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4T {tex}\times{/tex} sin 90
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  Fe = 4.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16N
Force acting on an electron is always perpendicular to direction of motion and towards center of the circular path and hence its path will be a circle. This force serves as the centripetal force, which explains the circular trajectory of the electron.
Centripetal force Fc = {tex}\frac {m v^2}{r}{/tex} ...(2)
By equating equation (1) and equation (2) we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}\frac{m \times v^{2}}{r}=4.99 \times 10^{-16} N{/tex}
⇒  {tex}r=\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{kg} \times\left(4.8 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{ms}^{-1}\right)^{2}}{4.99 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{N}}{/tex}
r = 4.2 cm.
Hence the radius of the path of electron shot into the magnetic field is 4.2 cm.

subject: Physics
question_text: In Exercise 4.11 obtain the frequency of revolution of the electron in its circular orbit. Does the answer depend on the speed of the electron? Explain.
question_html: In Exercise 4.11 obtain the frequency of revolution of the electron in its circular orbit. Does the answer depend on the speed of the electron? Explain.
solution_html:
Given:
Magnetic field strength, B = 6.5 G = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 T
Initial velocity of electron = 4.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 ms-1
Angle between the initial velocity of electron and magnetic field, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 900
We can relate the velocity of the electron to its angular frequency by the relation,
V = r{tex}\omega {/tex} ...(1)

We understand that the magnetic force on the electron is equal to the centripetal force on it, hence we can write,
Fe = Fc
e {tex}\times{/tex} V {tex}\times{/tex} B = {tex}\frac {mV^2}r{/tex} ...(2)
From equation (2) we can write,
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} eB = {tex}\frac {mV}r{/tex} ...(3)
Now, by putting value of V from equation (1) in equation (3)
{tex}e \times B=\frac{m \times r \times \omega}{r}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} e {tex}\times{/tex} B = m {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\omega{/tex} ...(4)
We know that {tex}\omega{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}
Putting in equation (4), we have
{tex}\nu=\frac{B \times e}{2 \times \pi \times m}{/tex} ...(5)
Now, by putting the values in equation (5) we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\nu=\frac{6.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{T} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}{2 \times \pi \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}\nu{/tex}= 18.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106Hz
Hence, the frequency of rotation is 18.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 106Hz.
Note: The frequency of rotation is independent of the initial velocity of the electron.

subject: Physics
question_text: A circular coil of 30 turns and radius 8.0 cm, carrying a current of 6.0 A is suspended vertically in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of magnitude 1.0 T. The field lines make an angle of 60° with the normal to the coil. Calculate the magnitude of the counter torque that must be applied to prevent the coil from turning. Would your answer change if the circular coil in (a) were replaced by a planar coil of some irregular shape that encloses the same area? (All other particulars are also unaltered.)
question_html:
1. A circular coil of 30 turns and radius 8.0 cm, carrying a current of 6.0 A is suspended vertically in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of magnitude 1.0 T. The field lines make an angle of 60° with the normal to the coil. Calculate the magnitude of the counter torque that must be applied to prevent the coil from turning.
  2. Would your answer change if the circular coil in (a) were replaced by a planar coil of some irregular shape that encloses the same area? (All other particulars are also unaltered.)
solution_text: Given, N = 30, I = 6.0 A, B = 1.0 T, {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 60° r = 8.0 cm = {tex}8 \times {10^{ - 2}}m{/tex} Area of the coil, {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{22}}{7} \times {(8 \times {10^{ - 2}})^2}{/tex} {tex}A = 2.01 \times {10^{ - 2}}{m^2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\tau = NBIA\;\sin \alpha {/tex} {tex} = 30 \times 6.0 \times 1.0 \times (2 \times {10^{ - 2}}) \times \sin 60^\circ {/tex} {tex}\tau = 30 \times 6 \times 1 \times 2 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \times {10^{ - 2}} = 3.12Nm{/tex} Since the torque on the planar loop does not depend upon the shape, in this case the area of the loop is the same, hence, the torque will remain unchanged.
solution_html:
1. Given, N = 30, I = 6.0 A, B = 1.0 T, {tex}\alpha{/tex}= 60°
    r = 8.0 cm = {tex}8 \times {10^{ - 2}}m{/tex}
    Area of the coil, {tex}A = \pi {r^2}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{22}}{7} \times {(8 \times {10^{ - 2}})^2}{/tex}
    {tex}A = 2.01 \times {10^{ - 2}}{m^2}{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\tau = NBIA\;\sin \alpha {/tex}
    {tex} = 30 \times 6.0 \times 1.0 \times (2 \times {10^{ - 2}}) \times \sin 60^\circ {/tex}
    {tex}\tau = 30 \times 6 \times 1 \times 2 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \times {10^{ - 2}} = 3.12Nm{/tex}
  2. Since the torque on the planar loop does not depend upon the shape, in this case the area of the loop is the same, hence, the torque will remain unchanged.
